In Thom’s Directory we find that at 58 Claremont
Terrace, Harold’s Cross, are domiciled the following :—
Viggo Schwartz, Expert; The Continental Agency Co.;
J. Hanson Wendleboe. Now who is Mr. Schwartz®
What is The Continental Agency Co.? and who is Mr.
Wendleboe? We have a card before us containing the
following : —* Telegrams, Organise ;” Telephone, 1090 ;
Viggo Schwartz, Egg and Poultry Market Expert, Trish
Agricultural Organisation Scciety, Ltd., 22 Lincoln
Place, Dublin.””  Was Mr. Viggo Schwartz of 58 Clare-
.mont Terrace, expert to the Irish Agricultural Organi-
sation Society, Ltd., imported for the benefit of
the Irish Egg and Poultry Industries? And if so, is
he entitled at the same time to run an agency for a

- Continental firm, as it is a curious thing that Dutch
margarine and other foreign stuff should be canvassed
for from the address of an expert of the 1.A.0.S.
"We have before us a printed leaflet of a firm
‘by the name of J. A. Van Den Bosch and
. Zonen, Margarine Manufacturers, Alkmaar, Holland,
Tt is endorsed with an india-rubber stamp— Peterson
Bros., 58, Claremont Terrace, Tarold’s Cross, Dublin.””

We have before us a leaflet of one Carl Bayer, Shipper
of Best Selected Fresh and Pickled Danish Bggs. We
read on it—Peterson Bros., Harold’s Cross, Dublin,”
.and it is india-rubber stamped—" Peterson Bros., 58
. Claremont Terrace, Harold’s Cross, Dublin.”” We read
—_«Below I have much pleasure in handing you my
~prices of Danish eggs (subject to being unsold and fluc-
tuations of market) for shipment next week, Your os-

Bayer of Copenhagen, concerning Danish eggs and Rus-

Company (J. Hansen ‘Wendlehoe) 58 Claremont Tor-

Thom’s Directory we find that the * Continental Agency
+Co, shares 58 Claremont Terraco with Viggo Schwaxtz,
. Expert, and turning to the printed card before us, we

find that Viggo Schwartz is Egg and Poultry Market
Expert to the Irish Agricultural Organisation Society,
Ttd. What is it all about? Perhaps the organ of the
Trish Agricultural Organisation Society, Ltd., the Irish
I omestead, would throw a little light on the matter!

Tho Rathmines Commissioners are a collection of sly
dogs; and old Mr. Edmundson, the Chairman, looks so
innocent and bland all the time! Old Mr. Edmundson
voted against the proposed Irish Language Classes ab
the Rathmines School of Commerce, cven though, for
the good of his dye works, he advertised them partly in
Trish! Rathmines is one of the most exclusive z_md
bigoted Corporations in Treland. It is now a long time
since we let in the light on the  colour”’ of its official
staff, and made the name of the Rathmines’ Commis-
sioners synonymous with Saved ”’ Ascendancy all over,
the country. It will be remembered that the 21 Com-
missioners are elected by two wards, one returning nine
and the other twelve members. The Ascendancy having
the machinery in their hands, do practically what they
like, and an odd Papist that they may be put on one of
the tickets can be put off if he turns out unruly. An
effort was made to have the division cut up into five
wards, and the Dublin County Council, after an en-
quiry had been held, recommended that that reform
should be carried out. The notorious bigot, the Solici-
tor-General, a law officer of the Crown, had a brief for
the Commissioners, and some legal quibble or other
was raised and the cause of Progress was obstructed by
the Rathmines’ antiquarians.

This exclusive Board, a lurid example of ‘“Saved’
Ascendancy, would like to take Terenure under its ex-
clusive wing. If they got their ascendant paws on
Terenure there would be no doubt a few more public
jobs that they could vote away to their Sourface friends.

The exclusive Commissioners attempted to get a Bill
through Parliament and failed. We understand that
the promoters of that Bill are responsible . for the ex-
penses—several thousands of pounds. They are mak-
ing a desperate second effort to get a Bill through.
Amongst many other matters, they want power to pay
the costs of the last attempt! Poor Mr. Edmundson,
the innocent and bland, does not want to be called upon
to put his hand in his pocket.

Well, a Ratepayers’ meeting is a necessity in the
proceedings that lead up to promoting this Bill. And
tho ratepayers’ meeting was held last week. M.
Michael Sullivan, a mild-mannered gentleman whose
name nevertheless raises the ire of Brother Goulding, is
a ratepayer of Rathmines. He heard of the meebbang
by chance. Tt is clear that the sly dogs of the Rath-
mines Urban Council must have done their part of the
work very quietly when one so elert as Mr. Michael
Sullivan only heard of the meeting by chance. The
innocent and bland Mr. Edmundson and his friends did
not send round a town crier with a bell announcing the
meeting; they evidently kept the whole matter as
quict as they legally could. -

But, alas, Ascendancy has not such an easy ti
it had prioi' to September, 1900. asy time as

At ten o’clock on a raw November morni
Sourfaces gathered in the Rathmines Town H‘:lgl’ (1?\1:
others gathered also). The innocent and bland old
Mr._Eduiuudsnn was in the chair, but Mr. Michael
iu}lhvam, not beloved of Brother Goulding, was in the
b )

- To make a long story short, when Mr. A. M. Sulli-
van, B.L., had laughed at the chances of the Bill
getting through next session, and had protested against
the waste of the Ratepayers’ money, Mr. Michael Sul-
livan got up and raked the Urban Council for its in-
tolerance. He referred to the manner in which the
intolerant Commissioners met the effort to divide the
district into five wards. The innocent and bland old
Mr. Edmundson complained that Mr. Sullivan’s refer-
ences to the wards question was out of order. Of
course it was; overything is out of order that ruflles
the slimy waters of Ascendancy; if a Sourface wanted
a public position in Rathmines, we have no doubt it
would be ““bigotry > for a Papist to contest the matter
with him! My, Sullivan said he would be sorry to
vote for the absorption of another district into their
intolerant township.

Eventually the innocent and bland old Mr. Edmund-
son put the matter to the vote, and though in our
opinion the “ noes *’ had it, old Mr. Edmundson blandly
stated that the “ayes’’ had it. There were cries of
“poll,” but old Mr. Edmundson said—* The meeting
is over.”” The question is not over though, and they
laugh best who laugh last.

A correspondent writes:—“I am a constant reader
of your valuable paper and admire it greatly, but T
cannot help feeling somewhat hurt at your prejudice to
publicans. I admit the vast majority of Bungs are not
Nationalists or ‘Irishmen’ in the true sense of the
word: but can a publican not be an Irish Irelander?
Can he not encourage the sale of an enormous amount of
TIrish goods in his establishment besides liquor. I, my-
self, am the son of a publican and I claim myself to be,
to the best of my ability, an Irish Irelander. I en-
courage Irish manufacture in every line. Everything
I purchase as far as possible is Irish-made, and in my
father’s house Irish-made goods are pushed to the
front and exhibited, not hidden away as many shop-
keepers (not publicans) do. I am also a student of
my native language and can fairly claim I am doing
my share towards the revival of Ireland a Nation.
Another “Bung’s’’ son—a cousin of my own—was
Vice-president of a Dublin branch of Connpad na
Taevilgeand is now Hon. Sec. to a branch in the West
of Ireland, and was on the Committee of more than
one yeir . Hoping your readers will no longer con-
sider iv impossible for a Bung or his son to be Irish—
if any are so narrow minded—1p mipe, ete.

3 As our correspondent admits that “ the vast majority
of Bungs are not Nationalists or Irishmen in the true
sense of the word,”” we don’t think he need fall out
with us for our attitude towards Bung. We never
said, for we do not believe it, that a publican could
not be an Irish Irelander, and as for condemning a man
because he was a publican’s son—that would be merely
monstrous; we certainly never did it. Bung is a term
that some publicans don’t like, and that other publicans
take in the greatest good humour. The term was
specially meant for the class who don’t like it. As our
readers know there was, and still is, a type of publican
who was, and is, putting on airs by way of being a very
superior social and “tony’’ personage, and he badly
stood in need of a tap on the knuckles, and we gave it
to him by calling him Bung. He doesn’t like it.
“When we were youngsters we did not like getting a rap

on the knuckles when we were hold boys even though

we may have sometimse richly deserved it. ‘We really
think Bung owes us a debt of eternal gratitude for the
solicitude which we have shown for his moral improve-
ment, though, such is the perversity of human nature,

‘we are sure that we would stand no chance of getting

an illuminated address and a purse of sovereigns from

Bung whilst Sergeant Wink-the-other-eye was retiring

or leaving on promotion.  There are, we have no

‘doubt, many decent publicans, and also a great many

0 have wide margins for being a great deal more -

han they are; there is no reason why a man

My, P. J. Anthony Maher, Templemore, writes:—
“ Enclosed is G. 8. and W. Railway advertisement of
cheap tickets to the New Zoalapd v. Ireland Rugby
match, at foot of which you are informed that tickets
ave available for return up to Monday. One finds on
going to the ticket office this morning that the ‘cheap’
tickets are the usual Saturday to Monday ones. A few
Sundays ago this Company advertised a cheap excur-
sion (return-on-date-of-issue tickets) to a football match
in Thurles, and the fare was ninepence, while the week-
end ticket-by the ordinary train reaching Thurles at
12-40 (a considerable time before the match started) is
cightpence. Needless to say that the people aware of
the issue of week-end tickets to Thurles waited for the
comforts of the ordinary train.”’

.Therc was a Bung Feis at Belfast last Thursday
night. Bung, of Belfast, was, uno doubt, as
anxious to prove his tolerance as he was to prove
his “loyalty ” by toasting * The King,” Even
though the Belfast Bungs took The King '’ in their
arms, we are about to rap their knuckles. One Owen
M‘Mahon is the Chairman of Bung’s Trade Union up
there euphemistically called “The Belfast and Ulster
Vintners’ Association.”” One James Kilpatrick, ex-
Emperor of Bung in those parts, was the recipient of
a complimentary dinner.  About ninety per cent. of
those assisting at drinking the health of “ The King”’
at this function were “Idolators.”” We understand
a  University graduate, one Edward Comerton,
B.A., is the “energetic and painstaking secretary ’
of Bung’s Trade Union in the Black North,
and the local “ Nationalist > daily paper tells us that
“it was in no small measure due to his zeal in this
matter that the affair was the indubitable success it
was.””  One item on the menu was “ baked cod.”” What
about the poor “soaked cods’’—the unfortunate vic-
tims of Bung’s bars throughout the country?  Alder-
man Murphy, of the Cork Bungs, was present; so was
Robert Russell, Secretary to the Dublin Bung’s. Apo-
logies for non-attendance at this “ God-Save-the-King "
Bung’s dinner were received from Joseph Devlin, M.P.,
and Tom Sloan, M.P. The Nationalist daily, the Zris
News, tells us:— The Chairman said his first duty was
to propose the toast of ‘The King.’ He would ask
them all to fill their glasses, and drink ‘ The King’
(applause). The toast having been drunk, etc.”” The
Chairman, one Owen M‘Mahon, is, we may remark, a
¢ great Nationalist.””  During the night gramaphone
selections were given for the delectation of the Bungs.
One Ju-ja Gibson, whoever he is, we read “sang a song,”’
and a Carl Hoffman treated the Bungs to a “ conjuring
entertainment.””

The waters of Irish Ireland are rising. The flowing
tide is with us, and it is in vain that England’s Faith-
ful Garrison push on to the front with frantic attempts
to stop the Irish tide. Constables Hogg and Bagge, old
Mr. Edmundson of Rathmines, Brother Goulding of the
Great Sourface Railway, and the rest of the awkward
squad of West Britons can do their little best, but the
tide comes along unhéeding, levelling their little sand
heaps and sweeping away their petty obstacles. Our
readers are referred to the front cover for a pictorial
view of England’s Faithful Garrison’s attempt to im-
pede the progress of the Irish Tide.

In our references last week to the exhibits of Cork
we omitted to mention the stall of Messrs. M. H. Gill
and Son, Dublin,who showed a nice selection of their
publications, etc. Messrs. Cogan and O’Callaghan had
an exhibit of their Irish crown self-raising flour made
up with the Irish crown stamp on each bag. In our
contributor’s (Fionn Dapp)  allusion to Messrs, H.
Johnston’s exhibit, the firms name was accidently spelled
“Johnson’’ instead of Johnston.

Is there any large grocery establishment in the City
that does not also deal in the liquids associated with
tho Magic Button? We only know ono. That one is
Glendon’s Rotunda Market, in Great B}'itam Street,
where everything pertaining to the legitimate grocery
trade can be purchased; but there are no infoxicating
drinks sold for consumption on or off the premises. The
proprietor of these stores has his own ideas about the
drink evil, and so—let the sacrifice in profits be what
they may—he will have no commerce in thom. This

THE LEADER.

248

enterprise certainly deserves the fullest support, on its
merits, of those who deal in groceries and have con-
victions concerning the drink evil. Despite the exclu-
sion of traffic in drink their business has expanded, and .
the new premises remodelled and fitted up throug}lqul:
from designs by Mr. W. A. Scott, are a worthy addition
to the business houses of Dublin. The work was car-
ried out by Messrs Dockrell, Sons and Co., Ltd., who
secured the contract against the competition of t]ge legd-
ing English shop fitters. The proprietor has laid him-
self out to build up a big trade on thoroughly up-to-
date business lines, and without the aid of Mr. Bung.

Dublin Castle is, happily, making itself more and
moro ridiculous by its quixotic tilting at Irish names
on carts. The Castlo had seized apples before, but last
week in addition to apples they seized the carcase of a
sheep belonging to Mr. P. J. Geoghegan of 103 Lower
Leeson Street. Mr. Geoghegan had been guilty of the
crime of placing his name in Irish only on a cart of
his. Some West British Quixots of woeful counten-
ance bore down on that menace to the integrity of the
Empire, and a fine of 10s. was imposed. And when
Mr. Geoghegan laughed at the West British Knights of
woeful countenances, these heroic defenders of the Em-
pire waxed wrathful, and seizing the carcase of a sheep
they sold it by public auction. ~And the Law was vin-
dicated.

Would not Dublin Castle be more usefully employed
if it sent its police to look after British goods striding
round the Irish markets on the pretence that they were
native. There was the recent case of British blacking
by the name of “ Cooneys.”” Why don’t the police look
after these sort of things and not leave it to the
initiative of bodies like the Guardians of the South
Dublin Union to move in the matter. There was a
case of a firm supplying “ Cooneys,”’ a British-made
blacking, to the Guardians who had contracted for
Irish blacking. Our two enterprising blacking manu-
facturers have enough to contend against without this
sort of thing going on under the eyes of policemen whe
have such a keen sight for Irish on Carts.

PAORAIT writes from Claremorris: —

“ The Redemptorist Fathers have just closed a mission
here. It lasted for two weeks, and during the time an
immense amount of work was done by them. Their
work for Temperance was the most remarkable. Dur-
ing the mission the.Archbishop gave Confirmation in the
town, and spoke strongly on the Temperance question.
The Missionary Fathers threw themselves into the work
with all their energy. Besides what they did for tee-
totalism they founded the Anti-Treating League. The
people took up the League with determination, and
practically all joined the League. Many from outside
the parish joined. Over 3,500 badges were purchased
from the stall-holders.  Thursday and Friday were
fair days, and there was a total absence of drunkenness.
Even the strangers were impressed by the array of
badges and the attitude of the people. To the honour
of our publicans and shop-keepers teas and coffees and
cheap refreshments were to be got everywhere. Drun-
kenness is banished for ever if the Anti-Treating League
is upheld. And wlhy should it not? = All have taken the
pledge till next mission. There is to be a quarterly re-
newal of the pledge until next mission on some Sun-
day, and a meeting the evening of that Sunday. This
will be a great thing. But out fear is that the outsiders
will have a bad effect on our League. Outsiders will be
here often, and especially on fair-days. Will our people
and town hold out against so many? Therc is a real
danger here, then. Now why, may I not humbly ask,
do not all the surrounding parishes take up the Anti-
Treating League, and do what the Missionary Priests
and people did here? Why is the grand work done for
us, and by us to be destroyed and ruined by outsiders?
The Archbishop gave his blessing to the League.
What Clonfert has doue, why cannot Mayo do? Aye,
and why cannot Ireland do it? The Anti-Treatine:
League of St. Patrick will save Ireland if all unite.”

244 THE LEADER.

AN IRISH PASTORAL PLAY.*

S‘Lemm na ST0E0Z “ The Glen of Fairies,”, is the

* title of a beautiful little pastoral drama from the
pen of Miss Mary O’Kennedy, a distinguished member
of the Keating Branch of the Gaelic League, which has
been just given to the public. . In plot, in scenery, in
pictures of pastoral romance, in weird suggestiveness,
this little drama stands high. The language is simple,
and often beautiful. Much of the dialogue is couched
in a subdued vein of poetic imaginativeness. The inci-
dents described range from passages of human pathos
recalling the ballad of the “Babes in the Wood '’ to
the przter-human frolic of the dream-instilling retinue
of the Queen of Munster Fairies. In holding the bal-
ance evenly between pathos and frolic, in creating an
interest at once in mortal children and in the death-
less aerial denizens of the woods and glens, Miss
O’Kennedy displays no mean dramatic taste and in-
stinct.  She leaves us undecided as to whether we
should prefer to weep with the strayed children in the
lonely glen, or gambol with the fairy host in our
exuberance of spirits, and set out with them on the
mission of disturbing sleeping mortals with delusive
dreams.  The heart-broken mother who believes she
has lost her little boy and girl for ever excites our sym-
pathy: the majestic Cliodhna, Queen of the Fairies,
fills us with awe; Cliodhna, so grand and stately, is
not without the pity and the kindness that we acknow-
ledge as human. The old woman acts as a sort of
connecting link between the fantastic preter-human
life of the fairy hosts and the sorrow-seasoned human
existence of the mortal characters. The drama is
short, containing only three scenes. There is much in
the stage direction to suggest fairy land and enchant-
ment, mist and darkness, and magic wands being in
constant requisition. It is an ideal play for school-
children; the fairy host—an indefinite multitude—
giving stage occupation to a considerable number of
children.  One hears a constant complaint of the
dearth of plays suitable for schools or for juvenilo
audiences; and, indeed, the English plays really suit-
able for such audiences are very few. Let our schools
—primary and secondary—try an article of genuine
Irish manufacture in the matter of plays. In prepar-
ing this play for the stage the school children will be
learning Irish as well as learning how to act and recite.
And as to the audience, even if they be not familiar
with the language of the play, the situations are so
striking, the pastoral and przternatural pictures pre-
sented in it are so vivid, that they will not fail to be
deeply impressed. Miss O’Kennedy has an instinct
for the weird, the romantic, and the beautiful that is
rare in modern play-wrights. It is interesting to find
the Irish revival movement giving rise to female dra-
matists. Besides Miss O’Kennedy we have Miss
Sheehy, to whom I awarded the prize for an Irish
play a couple of years ago at the Oireachtas, and who
has since produced a well-known drama entitled,
Deapc Nocarve. Let us hope that as the movement
‘grows, even still better work will be forthcoming from
lady-writers. PADRAMT UA Omnnin.

SOME ORANGEMEN AkD THE IRISH REVIVAL.

URING recent times certain matters have evelved
somewhat as we anticipated. If as a matter of
fact the “Idolators’’ of this country are, speaking
generally, the Irish, and if the non-“Idolators’ or
“BSaved ’’ are, again speaking generally, Engiand’s
Faithful Garrison, then the revival of the Irish element
in Ireland must inevitably create curious situations.
It has already created them. The Irish being,
broadly, the “Idolators,’”” the Irish Revival means,
broadly, the revival of the “ Idolators.”” The “Zrish
T'émes, the Ezpress, the Belfast Neus Letler, and a long
string of such papers that represent at once Protes-
tantism and West Britons, are bitterly hostile te poli-
tical Home Rule and the Irish-Ireland Movement. But
as we have often said, there is nothing the un-Irish class
of people represented by these papers dislike more than
to be “out of it.”’

Now it is inevitable that an Irish Revival, under
existing circumstances, being necessarily, for the greater
part, a revival of ‘‘Idolators’’ would give the Wes
Britons pause, and according as the situation developed,
a certain question would give some trouble to their
“ consciences,”’ or to their ‘economic sense,’”’ which
may do duty for their consciences—that question is:—
Are we to be “out of it?’ It would go hard indeed
with some of them to fly even a bogus green ribbon by
way of being Irish; and we have no doubt that there
aro many heroic true-blue Planters in Ireland who
would go down with the falling fortunes of West
Britain rather than call themselves Irish, g

It is now a long time since we commenced to develop
this true situation; we did not make the situation, we
morely opened our eyes—a more or less rematkable
thing to do in this country—and found it. The situa-
tion is made up of the broad facts that the Catholics
are the Irish, and the non-Catholics are England’s Faith-
ful Garrison. The situation has been developing long
enough to produce some results. ;

A few fellows like Mr. T. Sloan, late of the stepg.af tla
Bolfast Custom House, do not-want to be “out of it,”
and they have, consequently, titivated themselves out
with some bogus green ribbons, and, as was to have
been expected, a few of the inevitable greenhorns on

T A ———

the Ivish side—whom, like' the poor, we haye always

with us—a few of these greenhorns are taking the

loanites as Ivish patriots in the making. Sloan and

Co. dimly sec ng that it is mot business for them to 1:70

palpable and undisguised foreigners in this country in

these days of Irish Revival, commence to spout ‘“Ire-
land, Ireland,”” and some of our overgrown but uudor:
expérieuccd callow youths, and some of our national’
papers dream dreams of converted Orangemen. Con-
verted Orangemen an ear !

"The whole situation is as plain as a mathomatical pro-
position. Sloan and Company are answering somewhab
to the pressure of the National situation which Leader-
ites all over the country have croated; and when a
foreign party within a country answer to legitimate
national pressure, it is, we suggest, a safe policy to
keep on that legitimate pressure. That is what we
would do in the circumstances anyway. Our general
view being that the ‘ Saved ’—and we appraise them
in this connection by their organs the “ Zrish’ T'imes,
the Eazpress, the Belfast News Letier and other public
manifestations—the “Saved '’ do not want an Irish
Ireland, then we say that the plain policy for the
“TIdolators >’ is to go ahead making an Irish nation on
their own account without them. Some of the shrewd
and cunning anti-national bigots who would not have
political Home Rule, and do not want equality of op-
portunity in University matters for ‘‘Idolators,” feel at
the same time that they don’t want to be “out of it;”
and if there are greenhorns amongst the Nationalists—
National sheep clothed in lion’s skins who would be
afraid to say ma-a-a-a before an anti-Irish Sourface—
if -there are greenhorns amongst the Nationalists,
Sloan and Co. may throw dust in the eyes of a few by
shouting “Ireland, Ireland,”” and by wearing a shoddy
greeu rosette on their coats.

Listen to Sloan late of the Belfast Custom House
steps, now leader, or one of the leaders of the “Inde-
pendent ”’ Orangemen. Talking to the members of
the Ballymacarrett Purple Star and Cregagh Young
Men’s Independent Loyal Orange Lodges, he said:—
“They in the North of Ireland and Independent
Orangemen were Unionists from the crown of their
head to the sole of their foot, and absolutely refused to
set up a Parliament in College Green; but, while they
were Unionists, they were mot of a hypocritical char-
acter. . . . It was their intention to place Protestan-
tism before party politics. In his capacity as member of
Parliament he endeavoured, irrespective of the Old or
the New Order, to do his duty to Orangemen and to the
Protestantism they represented (applause). If they
wanted the assistance of their Orange brethren in Eng-
land to save them from Home Rule, their English
brothers had also a claim upon their aid by doing their
part to save them from the priest rule which fvas
threatened by the Education Aat.”

There is an “ Independent ’’ Orangeman for you! He
is a Unionist from.the crown of his head to the sole of
his foot ; he wants no Parliament in College Green; he
intends to place Protestantism before party politics;
he wants to keep his English brother from “ priest rule *’
in Saxonland; and then turning round to our green-
horns, he says—“I am an Irishman;’’ and the green-
horns of “ superior,” as well as of ““ inferior,”’ education
shed tears, and see visions through the thick mist.

We recently exposed the marc’s nest discovered by

Wade Thompson, the Pervert Priests’ Protector; hoth
the “ Irish '’ Tvmes and the Fapress were caught in that
nest. Tt was none too soon, indeed, that the Catholic
Defence Society was gstablished, and we welcome it as
a beneficont force in Irish life. Even the “Indepen-
dent”’ Orangemen place Protestantism before party
politics, and won’t touch. Flome Rule, though, no doubt,
Mr. Sloan, late of the Belfast Custom House steps, and
his friends, smell burning faggots when they hear the
dreadful news concerning the establishment of the
Catholic Defence Society! ;

Reason is a precious thing notwithstanding the abuse
the word was subjected to whoen the French Revolu-
tionists attempted to turn it into a goddess ; Nationality,

though likewise susceptible of abuse, is a precious th
and a groat and fruitful idea. But we would like to
cry a halt to a tendency to abuse the word “Nationa-
lity ”” by some of those who would erect it as a thing
above, and independent of, religion—as if, logically,
anything could be above religion. Tt would be simple
cenough for some to use “ Nationality ’ as many French
Revolutionists attempted to use “ Reason.”” ‘“ Reason ’”
as a goddess was used o hit religion; and “ Nationa-
lity >’ may be engincered by certain people, aided by a
few well-meaning, but muddle-headed, greenhorns, to
hit the Church in Ireland, and weaken its wholesome in-
fluence on the country. We cannot conceive any real
conflict between the true interests of Irish Nationality
and the true interests of the Church; but we can
conceive—and we would like o put honest people
an their guard against them—we can conceive designing
people raising the catoh-cry “ Nationality ” as a lever to
squeeze and damage the Church.

It is denied—and on the face of it justly—that the
Irish Parliamentary Party is a purely Catholic body—
as a matter of fact, the Party contains several non-
Catholics. The United Irish League is ‘“non-sectarian,””
as the usual phrase has it—as if Catholicity could, logi-
cally, be a “sect”’—and the United Irish League cannot
formally move in any matter as a Catholic body. Now
admitting the sweet reasonableness of all this, let us
point out that the Catholic body—in its secular aspects
—is entitled to have an organisation of its own for de-
fending itself against secular injustices and tyrannies.
The United Irish League, the Gaelic League, and the
TIrish Parliamentary Party being—properly as we think
—what is styled “non-sectarian,” there is no ground
for objection, particularly in face of even the “ Inde-
pendent ’’ Orangemen placing Protestantism before
party politics—not to mention the existence of the ortho-
dox Orangemen and the Freemasons—there is no ground
for objection to the Catholics forming a distinct secular
organisation for the defence of legitimate Catholic in-
terests.  Of course it may smell of “ Manchester ”’ to
some, and of brimstone to others; but unfortunately
Catholics cannot live and bring up their families pro-
perly on cither metaphysics or on scavengerships in the
Belfast Corporation.

We welcome the foundation of the much-needed
Catholic Defence Society, and we urge every one of our
“TIdolatrous '’ readers to rally to its side.

ULY 4.-A heated debate in the Corporation on 
the question of the Main Drainage. 
Several 
Councillors express a disbelief in the existence of the 
drains, and ask to be shown them. 
The Lord Mayor 
(who was repeatedly accused of "unworthy tergiversa-
tion" during the debate) assures the Council that the 
Main Drainage is " nearing completion," and that the 
works will " soon be out of the contractor's hands." 
He is greeted with impatient cries of "We heard that 
befor.e." 
One Councillor accused his Lordship of hav-
ing dined with the contractor's Manager, whereupon a 
scene of great excitement followed, during which the 
Lord Mayor (Mr. Alderman Blather) exclaimed in a 
tone of passionate emotion that he was " willing to lay 
down his life in defence of the Main Drainage." Several 
members · of the Council shed tears, and a number of 
ladies were carried out of the gallery in a fainting condi-
tion. 
The Councillor who had accused his Lordship of 
dining with the Contractor's Manager admits that he 
had only heard a rnmour to that effect, aJld withdraws 
his accusation. 
The Lord Mayor then kissed the Sword 
and Mace, and swore that he would pledge his existence 
-" his all "-;-that the Main Drainage would .be com-

pletedv“ ab no far distant date.’”” The members of the
Council then sang “ God Save Ireland’ standing, and
the meeting terminated.

1909.

JANUARY 10.—A formjdable emeute takes place in the
scavenging department, and a dictatorship is proclaimed
ah_ ‘Whitehorse Yard, the Superintendent being dis-
missed, and Mr. Patrick Flanagan named as dictator.
He suspended the cleansing regulations until the Ist of
June, the streets not to be swept meanwhile, and all
brooms and mud-carts locked up. He further declares
that in future the scavengers shall not work on any wet
day, nor on the two days succeeding any wet day.

FEBRUARY 6.—The streets impassable with mud. The
strike at Whitchorse Yard still continuing, the Lord
Mayor, Aldermen and Councillors, with inany promi-
nent citizens, resolve to form themselves into an Emer-
gency Cleansing Corps. The project is opposed by the
Labour members of the Council, who - side with the
strikers.

FEBRUARY 8.—A sharp encounter takes place in Col-
lege Green between the Emergency Cleansing Corps and
the strikers, four Aldermen and an Italian warehouse-
man being put out of action.

FeBrUARY 9.—The Emergency Corps again take the
field. Flanagan, the Dictator, drives a mud-cart through
their ranks, brandishing a broom fiercely, and knocking
off the Lord Mayor’s hat. The Fire Brigade, which has
remained loyal to the Lord Mayor, is ordered into action,
and the strikers are routed, with many injured.  This
engagement took place in Lord Edward Street.  The
retreating strikers are pursued to Whitehorse Yard, and
there closely beleaguered.

FeBruary 10.—The scavengers capitulate, on condi-
tion of being allowed to march out with their brooms to
resume work. Flanagan, the ex-dictator, is exiled to
Inchicore, and Brophy, his adjutant, is banished to
Ballybough.

FeBrUary 15.—“ Vigilance’’ and “ A Ratepayer
writing to the papers, declare that the streets are dirtier
than ever.

MarcE 3.—A revolution in the Corporation. The
city declared a democratic republic, and all titles
abolished. Henceforth each member of the Council to
be called simply “ Citizen,”” and the Chairman to have
no salary. This causes many members to resign on the
spot.

AMarcH 10.—The civic republic turned into a des-
potism, and Citizen P. J. Bung is named dictator. He
decrees that all public-houses shall remain open for
twenty-six hours each day, except Sunday, when they
shall remain open for twenty-eight. He further decrees
that all “off ”” licences shall become “on’’ licences at
the holder’s pleasure.

MarcE 17.—The Dictator Bung decrees that any man
found sober at nine in the evening of this day shall be
fined a pound.

ApriL 1.—The rabble declare Bung a deity, and offer
him divine honours. He becomes crazy under the in-
fluence of his own power, and the flatteries of the mob.
and proclaims himself Emperor of the West, Lord of the
Setting Sun, Prince of the Peerless Pint, Chief Froth-
blower of the Earth, and Son of the Three Stars. He
causes medals to be struck in his honour, and distributes
Jarge quantities of ‘ casky ’’ porter to the crowd.

ApriL 3.—The Dictator proclaims himself Lord of the
North Lotts, Khedive of Irishtown, Keeper of the Slob-
lands, and Hereditary Marshal of Pimlico. e distri-
butes quantities of silent spirit and no-star brandy to
the mob, who hail him “ Allah!”’

APRIL 7.—A counter-revolution. Bung deposed, and
exiled to Dolphin’s Barn. Citizen Kelly scizes on the
chief power, and dictates to the city. All public-houses
to remain open only four hours each day, from fwo
o’clock to six o’clock a.m. No Sunday trade allowed.
Bonas giving city addresses to be hanged on their return
to the city. The Licensed Grocers’ and Vintners’ As-
sociation proclaimed as an illegal society.

May 1.—The conflicting parties in the Council arrive
at a compromise. The Lord Mayoralty to be restored,

1911.

OcroseEr 2.—A resolution passed, declaring that for

1912.

June 10.—The new public park at Fairview is opened
by the Lord Mayor, yho entertains the chief citizens at
luncheon in the pavilion. Lordship declares his joy
at being head of the municipality on this auspicious oc-
casion, and congratulates the citizens that the once un-
sightly sloblands are no more. He further announces
that the Main Drainage works are “nearing completion,”
and that they “ will soon be out of the contractor’s
hands.”” The time is fast approaching, said his Lord-
ship, when, as of old, before the base invader had sul-
lied the sacred soil of Erin with his feet, the Liffey shall
be seen rolling its crystalline waters to the ocean through
the regenerated capital of a free country.

JunE 11—Tt is announced that the Corporation intends
applying for a loan of £295,750 for “ additional works *’
in connection with the “new ’’ Main Drainage. These
include  perfecting the western out-fall works,”” and
the “ erection of a new pumping-station in the centre of
Grafton St.””

JuLy 7.—A new loan of £310,000 applied for in con-
nection with the Electric Lighting.  The rates being
17s. 8d. in the £ already, several large firms threaten to
remove their works to Clondalkin.

1913.

FeBrUARY 4.—Several persons with poles and lanteins,
and wearing long boots, are found exploring the neigh-
bourhood of Burgh Quay. They allege that they are
“looking for the Main Drainage,”’ but are dispersed by
the police.

FEBrUARY 5.—The Lord Mayor proclaims it felony for
“ unauthorised persons’’ to search for the Main Drain-
age. A youth caught looking down a man-hole is ar-
rested, but on representing that he had come with a
message to his father, who is working in the drains, ke is
discharged.

May 12.—Much sensation in the city on its being dis-
covered that the Lord Mayor has not drawn his salary
for two months.

May 15.—Upon its being found that the Lord Mayor
has fallen into a melancholia, a special meeting of the
Corporation is held, and a Commission of Regency ap-
pointed to discharge the Mayoral functions until his
Lordship recovers. The Commissioners named are
Alderman Blather, and Councillors Gabby and Spout.

JUNE 3.—The Regents, in discharge of the Lord
Mayor’s social functions, give a splendid banquet at his
Lordship’s charges. 5

JuNE 14.—The Lord Mayor recovers from his melan-
cholia, and threatens proceedings against the Regents, to
recover the cost of the late banquet.

June 17.—Much raillery directed against the Lord
Mayor on account of his threatencd proceedings.  His
Lordship is alleged to have given Californian champagne
at his only banquet.

June 19.—The Lord Mayor withdraws his proceedings
against the late Regents on his son heing appointed to a
Rate Collectorship.

1914,

FEBRUARY 6.—An acrimonious debafe on the Main
Drainage question.  The Lord Mayor endeavours to
pacify the Coulicil with an assurance that the works are
rapidly “ nearing completion,”” and that they “ will soon

failed on the North side, leaving that part of the city in

OcroBER 17.—The Lord Mayor mobilises a corps of

OcroBER 19.—The North side rising in revolt, expels

vengers, four battalions of Sanitary Inspectors, a squa-

OcroBER 20.—Several strong skirmishing parties, com-
pesed mainly from the Corps of Scavengers, are sent

occupying Nagle’s, Mooney’s, Gilligan’s and the Arch,
as important strategic points. But the main force,
weakened by the detaching of these parties, is hard
pressed by the Northern forces. The Mayoral com-
mander seeks to recall the four detachments, but they
decline to come, unless permitted to bring the entire con-
tents of the four fortresses along with them. The Nor-
therns press the four strongholds hard, but fail to expel
the invaders. The battle between the main forces proves
indecisive, and the Mayoral forces remain in possession
of Rutland Square.

Ocroper 21.—The Mayoral Government cuts off the
water-supply from the North side, and the Northerns,

peace are arranged; the electric light to be perfected,
and the Northern forces disbanded. His Lordship pro-
mises to establish a summer residence at Ballybough,
and declares that the new Main Drainage will specially
benefit the North side.

1915.

ing-station in the centre of Grafton St. will be “an

. May 5.—The Corporation, at the instance of the vic-

CHRISTMAS IN IRELAND.

OW far is our Christmas a native one?—is a ques-
tion which has often occurred to me. We have,
indeed, developed Irish Christmas cards in recent years,
and are doing a few other things in that direction.
But how far do the essential elements from the Christ-
mas gifts to the plum-pudding belong to us? To
Christmas there are, of course, two sides—the religious
and the social. The former is, of course. universally
celebrated throughout christendom, though in the East
a difference of calendar causes a difference of date. The
latter—the social festival—is mnot universally cele-
brated; in France, for instance, it scarcely exists at
all, and it is in fact of pre-Christian origin. The fes-
tivities devoted to that jolly old deity, Saturn, pro-
duced a period of gaiety and fraternity in December,
and the Church in this case, as in so many others.
giving a new turn to pagan institutions, adopted them
into her celebration of <hristmas. The custom of
overeating on the 25th December most naturally finds
its origin in pre-Christian times.

The religious side of Christmas is much the same in
Ireland as elsewhere, save that the most characteristic
ceremony—Mid-night Mass—has been abandoned.
We have no distinctive religious ceremonies of our own
except the Christmas candle, if that belong to us. The
disappearance of Mid-night Mass has, indeed, served
to render the religious celebration of Christmas little
different from that of any other holiday, and though
it undoubtedly lead to abuses, the dropping of so dis-
tinctive a Catholic custom seems a pity. I remember
attending it at Westminster Cathedral two years since,
and the ceremony was very impressive. On the social
side we have, of course, a great body of observances,
and it is cf these I would fain inquire how many are
our own. To begin with, let us recall what they are.

First come the English Christmas numbers You
are trying to persuade yourself that the chill in the air
is only temporary, and some of summer clemency is
still lingering in the weather, when they come to appal
you with their pictures of the bluff old squires at their
unending meal, and the indignant mail-coach stuck in
the snow. Next comes Advent, and coming home
some Wednesday especially hungry and expectant you
find to your dismay a Kriday’s dinner set before
you. And soon Christmas begins iu real earnest. The
crowd in Grafton St. thickens, and gets more excited.
The schools give forth their young, and, enjoying a
brief period of unwonted rest and cleanliness, fill the
city, and wear their cleanest caps and their best clothes
all the time. Meanwhile, Christmas in the shops
drags its slow, insincere coils through the columns of
the dajly press, and the anticipations of the panto-
mime, with triplex fairy glades and other surpassing
splendours fill the Saturday evening issues. 3

And now comes Christmas Eve. Ok, my country-
men, how we have permitted ourselves to be deluded
about Christmas Eye! By dint of reading of Dickens
and other English literature, we have, by an astonish-
ing process of English cerebration, come to believe that
Christmas is a merry occasion in Ireland. It is not.
No amount of the English mind can get over the fact
that Christmas Eyve is a fast day, and a fast day on
which you generally go to confession. You may, in-
deed, walk up and down, and enjoy the lamp-glare in

" DEMAND AND SUPPLY, OR SUPPLY AND
DEMAND.

R. W. F. COMBER, of Messrs. Corrigan and
French, London, discussing at the recent con-
ference the matter of how best to bring under the
notice of the individual purchaser the importance of
buying Irish goods, and a knowledge of the articles of
Irish manufacture which he can purchase, made some
interesting and ingenious remarks which, as they have
1ot been published in the daily Press, will be fresh and
new to our readers. He said:—

‘‘ It has long appeared to me, for many years prior
to the advent of the Industrial Revival movement, that
the stagnation of Irish commerce comes from a fatal
credulity in the oconomic heresy, that Demand creates
Supply. Now, demand does not create supply. On
the contrary, Supply creates Demand. In other words,
demand is the result, and not the cause, of supply.
Nobody ever demanded steam traction and the iron
railroad as substitutes for horsed vehicles and the old
coach roads. On the other hand, the supply was
strenuously opposed, and it was a long and severe
struggle for the suppliers, before they succeeded in
capturing the demand. Nobody demanded the super-
session of steam traction by electric haulage, but supply
has come along, and demand responds. To take a
homelier illustration, as explaining the question of how
to get Irish goods, which, as Mr. Russell very rightly
remarks, ‘is not so simple a question as one would
think.” If you were at this moment in the West End
of London, in those parts where the Wholesale Woollen
‘Warehouses are to be found, and whose centre is Golden
Square, you would see, at nearly every door, porters
with hand vehicles laden with sample cases. The wool-
len manufacturers from Yorkshire, to the West of Eng-
land, and Scotland, are up in town, submitting next
season’s designs of their woollen cloths. Next season
means next winter, twelve months hence.  They re-
ceived their orders for next summer’s goods in May
last.

‘“ Now, obviously, the merchants who are ordering
goods for next winter cannot yet have any demand for
them. No man has been to his tailor in the winter of
1905 to order his clothes for the winter of 1906. So far,
then, the fountain of production, which is the manu-
facturer, has had. to provide the supply to the merchant,
which has evoked orders. After the manufacturer has
secured as many orders as he can, or as he feels he re-
quires, he returns home, and prepares to fill his
machines and looms; and his workpeople are happy in
the prospect of stoady work for scveral months to come.
His anxieties are removed to the shoulders of the mer-
chant. Now the merchant who has ordered the goods,
has no demand for them. He has bought because he
knows that only by providing supply can he elicit de-
mand. He, therefore, has patterns of his purchase sent
up in advance of the goods from the manufacturer.
These he distributes to his agents or customers all over
the world. In due season his home travellers are
equipped with these patterns, and proceed to visit all
the principal, and in many cases the obscure, towns in
Iingland, Treland and Scotland. No demand has yet
been reached for even an inch of these goods, now being
merrily woven in the looms of the manufacturer.

“The traveller displays his samples before the tailor,
the woollen draper, and the general storckeeper. These
Jatter, for the same reasons which actuated the manu-
facturer and the wholesale merchant, buy; for they

- know, if they don’t provide a supply, it is impossible to

evoke from their customers a demand. This is the final

~ link in the chain of supply. The noxt step is on to the

limbs and loins of the weaver, the retail wearer or con-
sumer, who alone represents the factor of demand.

«From the foregoing it must be plain to everybody
that neither shopkeepers, nor tailors, not even the
large wholesale houses, are responsible for ﬂ‘m difficulty
in obtaining Irish goods, at all events, Irish woull.cn
goods.  Tho solution is in the hands of the Irish
manufacturers themselves. : e

¢ The producer,” says Mr. Wm. Field, MP, in his
admirable essay, published in the Exhibition pro-
gramme, ‘is the economic mainstay of national pros-
perity.’ &

““fTow many of these economic mainstays are there
from Irish mills showing next winter’s patterns to the
great wholesale distributors in Dublin, Belfast, London,
and other cities?

““If seed is not sown in season it is vain to look for a
satisfactory crop, and it is useless for a consumer to de-
mand from a shopkeeper an article which he has had
uo opportunity of acquiring.

‘“ Several friends have urged that lack of capital
would prevent Irish manufacturers from entering the
lists along with wealthy competitors.

““To this I reply that capital is always seeking em-
ployment, and where enterprise and energy, and dili-
gence manifest themselves, capital will flow thereto, as
surely as the needle is drawn to the magnet.”’

A BELFAST SCAVENGER'S STORY.

T sit beside my barrow

With my bucket and sprong at my feet,
And break my crust in sorrow

On the side of the silent street.

The houses in fog are rolled,

The flags with the frost are grey;
My fingers are stiff with the cold,

And my clothes with the crusted clay.

I have been at this work these years,
I could find nothing else to do;
If of plenty of jobs one hears,
There are plenty to look for'them, too.

I wasn’t without education—

But, being young when my parents died,
I had never a situation,

Nor even for one had tried.

You would think in a city so busy,
If one only kept a look-out B
In the papers, that ‘twould be easy,
For my likes to find work that would suit.

So it might ; but somehow 7 seemed fated
‘Where vacancies were to be late;

And where others would be if T waited
To be just then too hungry to wait.

I found, too, most firms required
Some suréty—such was their rule—

And not one of them but inquired
Where did I go to school.

And so on—still trying to be cheery,
I walked till my feet were sore—
To find myself worn and wear:
One eve near the Town Hall door.

All have heard of our Corporation,
And how in it Catholics fare;
So ’twas only desparation
Could make me apply for work there.

The young man in charge of the place
Kindly bade me the time of day,
But a smile came over his face
When he hoard what I had to say..

THE CONDITION OF ENGLISH POLITICS.

N all ages and in all nations the moral, intellectual

and social condition of a people is, in normal times, to
be found reflected in the type and character of its pub-
lic men. A just people will tolerate only just rulers—
a corrupt people will be ruled by those who are them-
selves corrupt; in prosperous and enlightened societies
the Government will be enlightened and progressive
—the Government of the ignorant will be composed of
knaves or tyrants, or of both. Now the present is a
normal time in Great Britain, and never surely was the
character of a nation better exemplified than in that
of its present race of statesmen. The national charac-
teristic illustrated by the readiness of the people to
change their religion centuries ago, still survives in the
readiness of their rulers to change their principles.
Lack of principle in responsible statesmen must at all
times be a danger to the State whose destinies they
control, but what is to be thought of a people which
tolerate and condones it? In all healthy societies a
representative constitution provides an available check
upon the first evil. Where the second evil prevails, the
societies are not healthy, and Decline and Fall are the
inevitable result.

To how great an extent these evils exist amongst the
rulers and the people of England at the present day is
a question, which, to be correctly answered, would re-
quire a more intimate knowledge of both than I ever
hope to possess. I am concerned only with pointing
to indications which go to prove that they do exist, and
these are the public performances of front-rank English
politicians, and the manner of their acceptance by the
Press and people of England. Statesmanship has been
reduced to the level of acrobatics, whilst the country
has looked on and applauded. Unblushing equivocation
passes ocurrency in the light of day as Diplomacy. The
whole ‘duty of the politician is not to say what he means
or mean what he says, but to say something that may
mean anything, but which in all probability means noth-
ing. The grave aspect of all this is that nobody is de-
ceived as to the game that is being played. The whole
thing is being done openly and is receiving the hall-

mark of public approbation. Of course the Liberals

wax indignant with the Tories for acting in this man-

ner; bub ovon before they have come into power they |
are acting in tho same manner themselves. Mr. Balfour
makes a spocch on the Fiscal Question. Nobody:
understands the meaning of what he has said, but’
evorybody understands that, whatever else he meant,

he meant that nobody should. Puzzle competitions in

such papers as 7'it-Bi's or Answers are out of date. The

British public, however, must be entertained, and Mr.

Balfour obliges with “The Premier’s Puzzle.””  Mr.

Chamberlain comes along and gives the public £és solu-

tion, which is that Mr. Balfour is a Protectionist. Lord

Hugh Cecil’s solution, on tho contrary, is that the

Ex-Premier is a Free Trader ! Other eminent Statesmen,

such as the Duke of Devonshire and Mr. Ritchie,

worry themselves over the puzzle for a time, but

give it up in the end, and retire from the Cabinet.

Similarly with the Liberals.  Sir Henry Campbell-

Bannerman makes a speech on Irish affairs. Up jumps
Lord Rosebery— That means Home Rule,” he says.

“Not at all,” says Sir Edward Grey; it means ab-

solutely nothing of the sort.” And so the game goes

on. Press and public set to to discuss with interest
the various puzzles propounded by the various States-
men. Statesmen! Are these Statesmen? Is there in
history a parallel for such blatant tomfoolery on the
part 6f responsible leaders? How can it be that Eng-
land not only takes these men seriously, but trusts her
fortunes into their keeping?

The lack of principle displayed on all sides is an evil
omen for England. However shameful and unprin-
cipled the government of her dependencies may have
been throughout her history, nobody will deny that at
home she has had great Statesmen who have been a
pride and a strength to her, and who, however fiercely
they fought for Party, sought to uphold the dignity and
the credit of statecraft. No doubt, the advice was al-
ways pretity generally acted upon which Pope caustically
gave in these lines: —

“ Get place and wealth ; if possible, with grace;

If not, by any means get wealth and place.” <
To-day, however, the first alternative has been frankly
abandoned. There are men —men like John Morley—
who are old fashioned enough not to stoop to the new
order of things. He has spoken on the Irish question.
His speech was guarded and diplomatic—unsatisfactory
in many respects—but it was at least honest, and not,
like so many other pronouncements, open to as many
different constructions as there are moves on a chess-
board. Such men, however, would appear to be a small
minority, and a rapidly diminishing minority at that.

The lowering of the standard of public life in Eng-
land is, needless to say, not a matter that Irishmen
have cause to fret over. It does not concern us except
in so far as it might affect the relations between the two
countries, and as England’s standard in her dealings
with Treland has always been the very lowest possible,
it is hard to see how we can be worse off in any event.

SOME PECULIARITIES OF IRISH MUSIC.

T has been remarked with a certain amount of truth
that in the discussion of Irish musical questions

we have a great inclination to travel in circles. There
seoms to be a dread of making definite statements, and
tho dearth of facts brought forward is unaccountable.
The undoubted peculiaritics of Irish (traditional) music
are handled in the most gingerly manner. The reader
unconsciously gets a feeling of insecurity, and begins to
doubt the most obvious truths. If, instead of much
theorising, we were given more facts the result would
not be so confusing. Let each person who is well ac-
quainted with any branch of Irish music come forward
and contribute the result of his personal experiences,
and we shall soon have a mine of important facts. These
facts can then be cautiously sifted, so that in time it
may be possible to decide with exactness and unanimity
what are the essentials of Irish (traditional) music and
what are not. It would then be feasible to lay down
conditions for the guidance of competitors and adjudi-
cators at the different Feiseanna throughout the coun-

The writer believes that the most marked internal
characteristic of Irish singing is the accurrence of quar-
ter tones.  Whilst this fact was in the possession of
cvery old-style singer, piper, and fiddler in the land, it
took a great deal of writing to elicit it. Even now the
existence of such intervals is not universally admitted.
Quarter tones are not peculiar to Irish music. They are
also found in the national songs of the Australian
Ma01'1§, and may possibly occur in the music of other
countries. also. Musicians noting down such peculiari-
ties have met with difficulties similar to those which
stand in our way here in Ireland. Original signs have
had to be manufactured to meet the case, all the re-
sources of ordinary musical notation having failed.
When similar means were suggested in dealing with our
own music, critics held up their hands in horror, and
said the thing was unhecard of. Even if these peculiari-
ties hold the same place in music that dialect does in
language, who will deny that they may have a con-
siderable value in musical literature?

No doubt the pipes have influenced our Irish singing
in the same manner. The peculiarity of the intervals on
th pipes has yot to be convincingly denionstrated. That
peculiar intervals are played by good pipers on the in-
strument cannot be gainsaid. ~ At pipe competitions a
frequent inquiry is, why do the pipers not play true in
the upper register? This out-of-tunencss cannot be ac-
cidental. If not it deserves to be investigated. So far
littlo attention has been paid to the pipes as being the
key to the Irish musical situation.

‘A fact, known to very few persons even of those who
have paid some attention to the matter is the possibility
of obtaining quarter tones on the pipes. This is an ex-
traordinary fact considering that the pipes are a wind-
instrument, and is due to the method of varying a note
by lifting the chanter of the instrument off the knee.
"This at once establishes the claim of the pipes as the
most suitable instrument for the correct rendition of
genuine Irish (traditional) music. 3

A noticeable feature of singers of traditional songs in
the West and South is the manner in which they wind
up their songs. Instcad of singing the last nobes of the
final versoin the same way as those in the preceding
verse, thoy finish by speaking the entive last line. This
may be the merest corruption, or it may be the survival of
a form of recitative now obsolete. ~ Your traditional
singer, particularly if he be an old man, works himself
up to a fine pitch of feeling towards the middle of the
song, and generally grasps the hand of his nearest neigh-
bour in his own. The two hands are then gently
swayed by the singer in time with the tune.

Even little facts are important in the discussion of a
subject about which so much ignorance prevails, and
every new scrap of information is a welcome addition to
our rather slender stock of knowledge.

ANTI-ENLISTING CRUSADE.

! e IN order that this important debate may be conducted

s L i the best possible way, I would appeal to
g “Imaal *’ (who is an over-prompt debater) to wait till
I have duly stated the case. His keen eye will observe
many openings for attack; and if he retorts on the in-
stant, he will lead me into the discussion of a scorc of
side issues. For example, in the debate opened by him,
Miss Milligan responded to his assertion—that a soldier
would not be debarred from enlisting by the idea that
he would ever have to draw that antiquated weapon—
the sword—against "his fellow-countrymen.  O’Neill
Crowley was alluded to, and instantly “ Imaal”’ left
tl}e xpaiu tracks, and treated us to one of his brilliant
diatribes on the subject of the evils of secret societies.
I may write on that subject at a later date, agreeing
less or more as to the evils and incfficiencies of the old
type of secret conspiracy; but just briefly now I must
object to his description of the Fenian Brotherhood
as a sort of society got up to brighten country life by
idle and aimless men. Let ‘“Imaal’’ turn to the his-
tory of the Famine years; let him read the burning
pages of John Mitchel’s last conquest—the story of the
expatriation of the Irish race in the fifties and sixties
of the nineteenth century. Let him read and think,
and he will need no explanation from me. And if he
thinks Fenianism an utter failure, let him study the
list of Parliamentary concessions which followed that
great, but unfortunately unaymed, defiance. Fe-
nianism may be said to have disestablished the Church
and reformed the land system. Mr. Gladstone openly—
perhaps foolishly—acknowledged that his conversion
was brought about by threats of force. And wherein
lay the power of Parnell? Not in his eloquence, nor in
his skill as a Parliamentary tactician, but in the fact
that he was recognised as the force which held Fe-
nianism in leash.
‘Well, enough of that. We are not now going to
argue the rights and wrongs of that question; but will
come at once to the discussion of the Anti-Enlistment
Crusade. It is perfectly true, as “Imaal’’ has stated,
that the Anti-Enlisting Crusade, on a large and or-
ganised scale, is a new thing. In Fenian times it was
thought rather good policy to enter the army with an
ulterior object in view. Nationalists thought it no
harm to fraternise with soldiers. And this policy was
in favour amongst the most determined Nationalists up
till very recently. I was in Dublin once on the occa-
sion of a time of a political disturbance and excitement
in company with those leaders and inspirers of Na-
tionalism, who are content to do the good work without
praise or fame.

As we walked along the street, a hussar in the uni-
form of a crack regiment passed up riding in the direc-
tion of Dublin Castle with despatches. A glance and
smile of recognition was exchanged with my companion,
who said—“ There goes one of my boys. Some day he
will hand over the despatches if I ask them.”” A short
time after I was in a country garrison town with the
same companion, and was enjoying his conversation,
when some soldiers came along. With a word of apo-
logy he left me, and went off on a recruiting mission
with a couple of them. The idea that men in Irish
regiments could be true to the Irish cause was chorished
and fostered with the best intentions, and was excuse
for a yast deal of enlistment. The Irish members of
Parliament, who took up the agitation in favour of the
wearing of the shamrock by soldiers, had this possi-
bility in yiew, and wanted fto stimulate patriotism
amongst Irishmen in the army.. I have myself had
amongst my national recruits an officer in the army,
who excused his presence there as having enfered it with
a view to ultimately serying Ireland.

Even among the regiments embarking for seryice in
South Africa there were hundreds—perhaps thousands
-—of foolish Irish boys, who thought that somehow or
other when they got to the seat of war with rifles in
their hands, their help would be given to the other side.
I was myself witness of the departure of a militia regi-
ment from a country depot about St. Patrick’s Day.

And now, perhaps, the names of some that had: that

Rudyard Kipling, to whom we should all feel in-
debted for his true and unvarnished pictures of military

ricks,”” and recounts the failure and tragic end of a

ciously pleased to discover an old army Fenian away
out in Australia still living out a sentence of transpor-
tation (and not a hair the worse for it); and the King
had pardoned him (and is he a hair the better of it?)
but look, you, all the Trish race is reminded of the fact
that the Fenians allowed a man to join the army; and
doubtless the powers that be, hope that the youth of
Treland will go back to the old disused methods of these
Fepians, and will not give heed to what is on the
posters, which are stuck up by the boys with the paste-
pots, which “Imaal” despises. So comes it that the
old transported army Fenian is pardoned, and the boys
of the paste-pot stand in the dock in Belfast, Cork,

MAREKETING OF IRISH PRODUCE IN ENGLAND.

7 HE producers and shippers of Irish agricultural
produce scored a point recently when the Vice-
President of the Department of Agriculture promised
the representative deputation appointed at a public
meeting held in Limerick on 25th October, that he
would— subject to the sanction of the Board ’’—ap-
point a representative to safeguard the interests of Irish
produce in the English markets. Tt is well known that
the necessity for making such an appointment has been
urged on the Department by those interested in the
matter for years past without effect. Tt was useless to
point out that the Danes, and all our other successful
rivals, are represented by competent and independent
‘Government officials, and that the success of our com-
petitors is due largely to the way in which these officials
‘boom, advertise and push the produce of their respec-
tive countries.  Sir Horace would smoothly reply :
“There is no comparison between these countries and
ours. They are dealing at a considerable distance with
traders who speak a tongue they do not understand,
who use a currency almost as difficult as the language,
and practise a commercial system different from their
own. They nced agents. We do not; and any ex-
penditure on this head would not be justified.”
Evidently the past year’s experience and the facts
put before him by the deputation have altered Sir

THE LEADER.

DECEMBER 9, 1905.

Horace’s views, and the question that now troubles the
Trish producers is—What sort of representative, or re-
presentatives, will be appointed? It is certain that
thoir duties will be heavy and exacting. They will be
expected to watch, detect, and report on the various
frauds which are helping to ruin the Irish butter in-
dustry, and out of which English rogues are reaping
huge profits.  The packing of common ¢ American
ladles ”’ into Trish firkins, and selling them as “ Inish
salt firkin butter;”’” the blending of farmers lump
butter with' perhaps a small proportion of creamery and
some foreign fats, and selling them as “Pure Irish
croamery butter;’’ the water-logging and milk-blend-
ing of Colonial and Siberian butters, and selling. them
as “Trish' creamery ’” or “Irish factory,”” are somie of
the more common means by whi¢h the good name of
Trish produce is lowered and foreigners enriched at our
expense ; but there are others not so glaring, perhaps,
although quite as insidious, and frequently more
destructive.

It is well known that Danish butter is boomed as
the best. It is carefully made, no doubt. A regular
supply of it is kept up during the winter months.
These points tell in its favour, and almost all leading
English grocers stock it as their leading line. t cer-
tain periods of the ycar, however, when it is scarce and
dear, many English provision merchants order hun-
dreds of tons of Irish creamery butter, get it packed in
kiels with locked hoops to resemble Danish casks, and
sell it as finest Danish butter. Irish creamery butter,
when not packed in boxes, is generally packed in kiels
or kegs with twigged hoops. The twigs are distinctive
marks of the Irish package; but these merchants who
adopt this system of substitution would not have the
twigged kiels at any cost. In this way the credit of
producing some of the best Irish butter goes to increase
the reputation of Denmark. The butter is sold at an
increased price, but the increase goes into the pocket
of the English rogue who sells it as Danish, and not
into the pocket of the Irish producer.

Complaints are frequently made by English cus-
tomers about the quality of the Irish butter they re-
ceive. Sometimes these complaints are justified ; some-
times the butter is only slightly faulty, and the com-
plaint is grossly exaggerated, while at other times there
is no justification for the complaint at all.

Reductions in prices are claimed, and if the Irish
producer or shipper has not an honest and energetic
agent on the spot to inspect the butters and inquire
into the complaint, he is completely at the mercy of the
customer. Even if there were no question of fraud on
Tvish produce, the services of official Government repre-
sentatives are necessary to keep the good name of Irish
produce before customers, to ascertain their require-
ments, report on their suggestions, transmit. their com-
plaints, if any, through the Department to the shippers,
to report on the quality of the butter shipped, and make
suggestions for its improvement in make or packing if
necessary, and to arbitrate in matters of dispute.

THE ERASMUS SMITH ENDOWMENT,— XIY.

What are the Trusts?

Who are the Beneficiaries?

EFORE answering these questions, it is necessary
to briefly recapitulate what I have done.

1. In 1657, Erasmus Smith gave by the Deed of that
date 2,874 acres in Galway, the Isles of Aran, and Ros-
common, to 18 Trustees, under the trusts set forth in
that Deed.

2. He sold the lands in the Isles of Aran to the Earl
of Arran, and gave instead of them to the Endowment
1,926 acres in Limerick, of which 592 acres were un-
profitable.

3. In 1664, he got certified under the Great Seal of
Ireland, and sent into England a Bill, in which he
stated that he had given to the Endowment 7,788 acres

DEecEMBER 9, 1905.

in addition to the 3,264 acres which the Trustees then
possessed under trusts somewhat different from the
trusts defined in the Deed. Those 7,788 acres which
ho had added, belonged to, and were in the possession
of other Adventurers at the time.

4. He got a clauso into the Act of Explanation
passed in 1665, enacting that the Trustees were to be
left in possession of all the lands they then possessed, for
the trusts given in the Bill; and that they were to get
profitable lands in Louth, or elsowhere, to compensate
the Endowment for any lands given in the Bill, which
should have been decreed away.

5. Seven of the original Trustees got from the Court
of Claims in 1666, a certificate confirming to them the
3,264 acres they then possessed, for the trusts set forth
in the Bill of 1664.

6. They got the Letters Patent of 1667, giving to
them the lands decreed to them by the Certificate of
the Court of Claims, for the trusts set forth in the
said Certificate, and in the said Bill.

7. They got by a second Certificate of the Court of
Claims, dated 21st of September, 1668, 3,067 acres in
Sligo, Tipperary and Limerick.

8. They got by a third Certificate of the Court of
Claims, dated 31st of December, 1668, 1,429 acres at
Dromhane, Tipperary. All the lands given by the
second and third Certificates were given to make up for
the lands mentioned in the Bill, and which were in the
possession of others.

9. Of the lands given by the second and third Cer-
tificates, more than 4,000 statute acres were bog and
mountain, whilst Erasmus Smith obtained for himself
of the best lands in Ireland, in Louth, over and above his
deficiency, about the same number of acres as were
given by these two Certificates to the Endowment.

10. By the Charter of 1669, he got erected the Cor-
poration, called “The Governors of the Schools,
Founded by Erasmus Smith, Esq.”” This Corporation
consisted of 32 members, and has continued down to
the present. To this Corporation were given all che

Rules all agreo in saying that all the children of all the
tenants on all the ostates of the Endowment are (a) en-

the Free Grammar Schools; and (b) to exhibitions at
the University. See Articles 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 11.

The three Certificates, the Lotters Patent of 1667, and

the Charter of 1669, agree in saying thab the same
class of “poor children’” arc entitled to money (c) for
-clothing in the schools, and (d) to apprentice fees. See
Articles 4, b, 6, 7, 8,
. The Chanter of 1669, tho Rules, and the Deed, by
implication, agree in saying, that all the tenant’s chil-
dren on all Erasmus Smith’s estates in Troland, are en-
titled to (e) free Primary and Intermediate cducation
in the Free Grammar Schools, and to (f) exhibitions in
the University. See Articles 2, 6, 7, 8, 9.

The Charter and the Rules agree in saying, that (9)
all the tenants’ “poor children’’ are entitled (%) to
money for clothing in the schools, and for apprentice
fees. ~ See articles 6, 7; 8, 9.

The three Certificates and the Letters Patont of 1667
estricted the tenants’ children on Erasmus Smith’s es-
es entitled to share in the Endowment, to those that
lived within two miles of the three Free Schools, re-

spectively. But he has withdrawn that restriction in

the Charter of 1669, and extended the Endowment to
the children of all the tenants on all his estates in Ire-
land, at what distance soever from the three Free

Schools they should dwell, or inhabit. He made this

restriction, just as he had suppressed three of the four

schools appointed by him in the Deed to be built. Thus
he gave himself the power to plant a school in any
locality in which there were large tracts of good lands to
be acquired. And by restricting the benefits of the
Endowment to tenants’ children living within two miles
of the school, he established a claim to get lands imme-
diately around the school, in order to bring the inhabi-
tants of those lands under the benefit of the Endow-
ment. He had acted thus clearly at Tipperary, where
he obtained the whole town and all the land around it.
He also most probably used the same artifice to get lands
at Drogheda, although I do not know how near his lands
come to the town of Drogheda. . This view is confirmed
by the fact, that, when he had obtained the lands, he
withdrew the restriction, as regards the tenants, and
put it on, as regards other poor, who were not tenants.

In the Deed, he directed that all poor children coming

to his schools from any distance should get free educa-

tion. In the Charter and Rules he restricted that
privilege to poor children living within two English
miles of his schools.

The three Certificates, the Letters Patent, and the
Charter direct that (7) £100 per annum should be paid
to Christ’s Hospital, London, and reserved to Erasmus
‘Smith a right of devoting a moiety of the surplus to
other charitable uses to be determined by himself. In
1681, he drew up a deed assigning this moiety to
‘Christ’s Hospital, London. His Trustees disputed his
right to make such a deed, and refused to pay. The
Report of the Commission of 1791, tells us, that the
‘Trustees afterwards settled the claim of Christ’s Hos-
‘pital by paying them: £2,000. So that the only foreign
claim on the Endowment now is £1000 per annum to
Christ’s Hospital, London. The Deed, the Certificates,
‘the Letters Patent of 1667, and the Charter of 1669,
«directed that after the tenants’ children in the first
place, and' twenty other poor children within two miles
of the three Free Schools, in the second place, had
been supplied with Exhibitions. in' Trinity College, or,
with clothing, or apprentice fees, exhibitions, clothing,
or apprentice fees might be given to “other poor
-children.”

The foregoing most clearly enables us now to answer
‘the question—Who are the Beneficiaries? They are
.all the children of all the tenants on all the Endowment
-estates, on all the estates of Erasmus Smith in Ireland,
-and the poor children, not tenants’ children, that live
within two English miles of the three Free Schools at

‘Galway, Drogheda and Tipperary ;-and after those three
«classes have been supplied, “other poor children,’’

So far there is no doubt, or room for doubt, as to
what are the clearly defined Trusts? and who are the
cloarly designated Beneficiaries? These clearly defined
Trusts, and these clearly designated Beneficiaries are
all admitted by Protestants. How could they deny
thom?  All this is confirmed—although it_needs
no confirmation—by the letter of the Trustees, dated
the 7th of August, 1681, in which they, relying on the
opinion of the Irish Solicitor-General of the day, tell
Erasmus Smith that they have not yet money to give
any Exhibitions to poor scholars in Trinity College, nor
to clothe or apprentice poor children. Thus we have
contemporary, and expert evidence of the truth of our
interprotation of the instruments of foundation, and
other authentic documents quoted.

And at the present day, Lord Justice Fitzgibbon is
most explicit in his approval of our interpretation of
the Trusts and Beneficiaries so far stated by us.

In Report 1886-7, p. xix., the late Lord Chancellor
Naish and Lovd Justice Fitzgibbon, say :—“ It must be
always remembered that the greater number of Endow-
ments with which we can deal are of private origin; and
are restricted by the intentions of the founders to par-
ticular classes, or particular localities.”” On the 9th of
November, 1889, the Secretary of the Educational En-
dowments (Ireland) Commission was instructed by Lord
Chancellor Naish and Lord Justice Fitzgibbon, to pre-
pare a draft scheme for the Erasmus Smith Endowment,
on the basis, part of which was the following :— That
it is an Endowment of private f.:'ndation, and, there-
fore, the scheme must have regard to the founder’s in-
tentions.”” They both have thus admitted that it is a
foundation of private origin. And Lord Justice Fitz-
gibbon, in his reply to Mr. Bodkin, K.C., Rep. 1886-7,
p- 308, has admitted that it has been restricted by the
founder to “ particular classes,’”” and “ particular locali-
ties.””  For he said :— It seems perfectly clear that the
particular bounty which Erasmus Smith wanted to give
was higher or Grammar School education. bk o
Have we evidence that the tenants on the Limerick and
Tipperary Estates want that education? I caiu under-
stand your claiming the benefit of the Endowment for
these tenants provided they are in a position to take
advantage of it.”’ -Again he says (ibid) :— Is there any
reason to suppose that Grammar School education is
wanted for those tenants?’ e thus admits that the
tenants on the Tipperary and Limerick Estates are en-
titled to”Grammar School education, and that Erasmus
Smith has left or “restricted >’ his Endowment to the
particular class of his tenants and the particular locali-
ties of his Estates. But he says that Grammar School
education is too high for them, is not wanted for them
When he had got the most conclusive evidence that
Grammar School education was wanted for them, he
changed his tune and fell back on the alleged founder’s
intention.

Again, in his judgment, Rep. 1894; P. 244, having
stated that he would admit Nonconformist Protestants
to the Endowment, he says:—* Having regard to the
